metadata.title: Same Text, Better Scaffold
metadata.subtitle: Access Scaffolding vs. Text Substitution in ELA
metadata.source_chapter: Agent Skills for K-12 Teachers (Anthropic) — k12-lesson-differentiation
metadata.description_blurb: Giving below-level readers a simpler text closes the door. Giving them the same text with access scaffolds opens it. This video shows the split: annotated passage on the left, clean passage on the right — identical words, different support structure.

beats.narration_text: Merhaba, I'm Liam — in for Bear. In ELA, the differentiation trap has a specific name: text substitution. A student struggles with the grade-level reading, so you give them an easier book. Problem solved — until you realise you've just locked them out of the grade-level conversation. Today's principle: same text, different support structure.

props.command: My below-level readers can't access the grade-level text. What's the difference between giving them easier text versus scaffolding the same text?

beats.narration_text: A passage on the First Amendment — the same words in both columns. Phase one: it appears full width, one text for everyone. Then the split. Left column, Below tier: the same passage, now bordered in terracotta, with four access scaffolds layered beneath it — a reading protocol asking students to circle the main idea and underline evidence, a vocabulary gloss for amendment and ratified, and a query prompt. Right column, At tier: the same text, clean, with a single analytical question at the bottom — identify the author's central argument. Identical words. Different entry infrastructure.

The scaffolds do three things. They break the reading act into explicit steps, so the student knows what to do while reading. They pre-teach vocabulary that would otherwise block comprehension. And they reduce the working-memory load of tracking everything at once. What they do not do is lower the conceptual target. Both students are still reading about First Amendment rights and building toward an argument about the author's intent.

props.artifactHeading: What scaffolds do — and don't do
artifactLines: Explicit reading protocol (circle main idea, underline evidence, flag unknown words)
Vocabulary glossing — block words removed before they derail comprehension
Working-memory relief — fewer things to hold in mind at once
Lower the conceptual target — the text stays grade-level
Change the words — both tiers read the exact same passage
Replace thinking with answers — the scaffold guides, not answers
Both tiers: same passage, same discussion, same author's argument.

beats.narration_text: A scaffold and a crutch look identical from outside — both feel like help, both raise performance while the support is on. They're opposite in what they leave behind. A scaffold lets a learner perform slightly beyond current ability while still doing the cognitive work, and it fades as competence grows. A crutch performs the work in place of the learner. The test: what happens when you remove the scaffold? If comprehension collapses, the scaffold wasn't building capacity — it was performing the access.

props.sparkLine: Remove it. Does the learning hold? That's the whole test.

beats.narration_text: The fading schedule is the mechanism that separates the two. Once students can access the passage with vocabulary support, the next version removes one scaffold at a time: first the glossary, then the sentence starters, then the structural markers. The goal is the same passage, unscaffolded, read by students who now have the skills to manage it. If the scaffold never comes off, you haven't built a bridge. You've built a detour around the reading.

props.sparkLine: One scaffold at a time, until the text stands alone.

beats.narration_text: Claude can build an access-scaffolded version of any text passage in one prompt. Give it the passage and the tier description — below-level readers who need vocabulary support and a reading protocol — and ask for the scaffold layer separately from the text. That way the original passage stays intact, and you can verify the words haven't changed before handing it to students.

props.artifactHeading: Access scaffolds open the door — they don't substitute for it.
artifactLines: BELOW (Access Scaffold): Same First Amendment passage +
Reading protocol: circle the main idea, underline evidence, flag unknown words.
Vocab gloss: amendment = change to law; ratified = officially approved.
AT (No Scaffold): Same passage + Identify the author's central argument.
Verify: words are identical in both versions before distributing.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take any reading passage from your next ELA lesson. Paste it into Claude and ask it to generate a scaffolded version for below-level readers — including a reading protocol, a vocabulary gloss for the two or three hardest words, and one comprehension anchor question. Then paste both versions side by side and confirm: is the text identical? If the scaffold replaced words or shortened the passage, push back and ask it to try again with the original intact.

props.command: Here is a reading passage. Generate an access-scaffolded version for below-level readers: add a reading protocol (circle main idea, underline evidence, flag unknown words), a vocabulary gloss for the two hardest words, and one comprehension anchor question. Do not change any words in the passage — the text must be identical.
